foodForestSites.notes: Real DUG spring-2023 food forest, but on an active DPS middle-school campus (Capitol Hill). School sites restrict public picking, so open harvest is not confirmed. Conservative Unverified.

foodForestSites.name: Glenbrook Greenhouse Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG food forest on a Denver Public Schools site
foodForestSites.notes: Real DUG spring-2023 food forest, but on a Denver Public Schools greenhouse site (Marston area, ~4900 S Field Way). School-district land, likely access-restricted. Conservative Unverified.

foodForestSites.name: Maxwell Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: Unverified, not in DUG food forest network
foodForestSites.notes: Not found in any DUG food-forest planting article (2022/2023) or directory. Batch point sits on Maxwell Elementary, Montbello, a school community garden. No authoritative source names a public Maxwell food forest.

foodForestSites.name: Edgewater Kaizen Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: Go Green Locally listing + DUG food forest program ethic
foodForestSites.notes: Real DUG public food forest, Kaizen Food Rescue collaboration near 25th Lane and Marshall St, Edgewater. On partner/church-adjacent land but publicly labeled a food forest; DUG program harvest ethic applies (food only).

foodForestSites.name: Vista Peak Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG volunteer/community-garden listing + Aurora Public Schools siting
foodForestSites.notes: Sits on the Vista Peak Prep (Aurora Public Schools) campus. Authoritative sources describe a 30-plot community garden with community beds, not a confirmed open-harvest public food forest. Food-forest label appears overstated.

foodForestSites.name: GES Food Forest (Globeville/Elyria-Swansea)
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG volunteer listing + GES Coalition/Tierra Colectiva coverage
foodForestSites.notes: Community-owned land trust food forest (Tierra Colectiva/GES Coalition with DUG), around 4798 Josephine St, Denver 80216. Explicitly free and open to neighbors. Young planting from 2024, may be lightly productive.

foodForestSites.name: Bradley International Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: Civil Eats coverage (repost) + DUG food forest program
foodForestSites.notes: Named as one of DUG's food forests, but on the grounds of Bradley International School (Denver Public Schools). Public harvest access at an operating K-8 campus is unverified. Cited source is a Civil Eats repost.

foodForestSites.name: Greenway Food Forest
foodForestSites.notes: Described as Greenway Community Garden (Greenway Park) with a new food-forest planting. No site page confirms open public harvest; community-garden context. Batch coordinates place it in the Aurora area.

foodForestSites.name: STEAM Academy Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG Food Forest Directory (on active DPS school grounds)
foodForestSites.notes: Confirmed in DUG ArcGIS directory (2900 Richard Allen Ct, Skyland, est. 2024). Site is Robert F. Smith STEAM Academy, an active DPS grades 9-12 school. Public harvest access unverified; marked Unverified to avoid trespass.

foodForestSites.name: Lake Middle School Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG Food Forest Directory (on active DPS school grounds)
foodForestSites.notes: Confirmed in DUG directory (1820 Lowell Blvd, est. 2025) as a real food forest, but sited on Lake Middle School, an active DPS campus. Public harvest access unverified. Conservative Unverified.

foodForestSites.name: Crumley Park Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG Food Forest Directory + food-forest harvest ethic
foodForestSites.notes: Confirmed in DUG directory (3400 W Cedar Ave, Barnum, est. 2025) and DUG naming article; open community food forest where neighbors pick fruit. Coords match batch.

foodForestSites.name: Newlon Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG Food Forest Directory (on active DPS school grounds)
foodForestSites.notes: In DUG directory (361 Vrain St, est. 2025), but that address is active Newlon Elementary (DPS Pre-K-5). Public harvest access on school grounds unverified. Conservative Unverified.

foodForestSites.name: Martinez Park Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: DUG Food Forest Directory + food-forest harvest ethic
foodForestSites.notes: Confirmed in DUG directory (4051 W 9th Ave, Villa Park, est. 2025). DUG describes its food forests as open for anyone to pick berries or nuts. Community food forest.

foodForestSites.name: Sanderson Gulch Food Forest
foodForestSites.foodSourceLabel: Denver Urban Gardens Food Forest Directory + food-forests program page
foodForestSites.notes: Confirmed in DUG's official ArcGIS-backed Food Forest Directory (feature service Food_Forest_Directory_, ObjectId 29), one of 29 listed sites. Program harvest ethic at dug.org/gardens-food-forests/food-forests/ (pick ripe fruit, take only what you need). Default non-food status kept unknown; craft/fiber not covered by the food invitation.

foodForestSites.name: Old West Church Food Forest
foodForestSites.notes: Real BFFC food forest but on private church land (a churchyard Garden Raising) with a donation model; own page states no open-harvest policy. Conservative unknown.

foodForestSites.name: Savin Hill Wildlife Garden Food Forest
foodForestSites.notes: Listed BFFC land-trust site but its own page frames it as a wildlife/native-habitat garden, not a harvest food forest. No open-pick statement, so unknown.

foodForestSites.name: Hope Garden
foodForestSites.notes: Own BFFC page names it a community food forest open to the public; harvest inferred from BFFC edible-public-park ethic. Some raised beds are community-tended.

foodForestSites.name: Leland Cooperative Garden Food Forest
foodForestSites.notes: BFFC Coalition site. Own page: cooperative garden, no individual plots, everything open for all to use; harvest with gratitude. Food forest label loose but open-harvest supported.

foodForestSites.name: Frederick Douglass Peace Park
foodForestSites.notes: BFFC Coalition public park (2023 renovation) with food-forest plantings. No explicit pick statement on page; harvest inferred from BFFC edible-public-park ethic.

foodForestSites.name: Egleston Branch Library Food Forest
foodForestSites.notes: On Boston Public Library branch grounds; no individual BFFC page, so cited to BFFC /locations directory plus gardensofegleston.org and network harvest ethic. Reachable cherry-tree fruit confirmed.

foodForestSites.name: Sherman Street Green Space / Fairy Hollow
foodForestSites.notes: Community-run semi-mature urban forest, BFFC member since 2024, described as a public edible park. Harvest inferred from BFFC edible-public-park ethic.

bronxRiver.sourceLabel: NYC Parks: Foragers in the Foodway (Concrete Plant Park)
bronxRiver.popupText: The Bronx River Foodway at Concrete Plant Park is NYC's first legal public foraging site, an edible food forest created by NYC Parks and the Bronx River Alliance. The public is invited to harvest ripe plantings for personal use. Take only a fair share, and leave the plants healthy for others and wildlife.
bronxRiver.evidenceQuote: Come outside and learn how to forage alongside NYC foragers and herbalists at the Bronx River Foodway!
bronxRiver.notes: Strongest primary source read: NYC Parks' own "Foragers in the Foodway" program page (nycgovparks.org), a recurring June–September series; the July 18, 2026 instance is live/upcoming as of today. NYC Parks, the agency whose 56 RCNY / policy otherwise prohibits foraging citywide, here explicitly invites the public to forage and references participants' "harvests" ("...learn the various ways to preserve and use their harvests"). This clearly beats the current citation: the Bronx River Alliance foodway page (bronxriver.org/visit-the-river/explore-the-greenway/foodway) and the permanent NYC Parks history/highlights page (nycgovparks.org/parks/concrete-plant-park/highlights/19764) both only say the Foodway "offers access to a variety of edible plants" and invite you to "take a stroll," stating no harvest policy. Corroborating NYC Parks primary source: the "Foragers Gathering" event page explicitly contrasts "Current NYC policies limit public foraging practices" with having "foraged raspberries at the Bronx River Foodway," framing the Foodway as the exception. Secondary confirmation that it is NYC's first/only place to forage "freely and legally": Civil Eats, Salon, NYC Food Policy Center (Hunter College), and Urban Omnibus ("A New Harvest"). Caveats: the event URL is dated (part of a recurring series, so a specific instance may expire); if a permanent NYC Parks link is preferred, the highlights/history page is stable but omits harvest wording, so I chose the event page for its explicit harvest-invitation evidence. NYC Parks' framing centers guided/community foraging of the site's plantings; the site's existence is not blanket permission for every species, consistent with the project's occurrence-is-not-permission stance, but harvesting ripe plantings for personal use is the Foodway's explicit designated purpose.
